passage: Neuroscientists studying sleep and cognitive performance measured reaction times in participants after different sleep durations over a five-day study. They claim that cognitive impairment from sleep restriction is cumulative: while a single night of 5-hour sleep produces modest slowing, the deficit compounds over successive restricted nights, and the cumulative impairment after five nights of restricted sleep exceeds what a single night of total sleep deprivation produces.
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the figure to support the neuroscientists' claim about cumulative impairment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Participants restricted to 5 hours of sleep showed average reaction times of 285 ms after night 1, 310 ms after night 3, and 362 ms after night 5, while a single night of total deprivation produced a reaction time of 340 ms -- lower than the 362 ms after five restricted nights.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After one night of 5-hour sleep, reaction times averaged 285 ms compared to the 260 ms baseline, a modest 25 ms increase that suggests minimal initial impairment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A single night of total sleep deprivation produced reaction times of 340 ms, which was 80 ms slower than the 260 ms baseline, demonstrating the severe impact of complete sleep loss.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The 8-hour control group maintained reaction times between 260 ms and 265 ms throughout the five-day study, showing no significant deterioration with adequate sleep.

explanation: The claim has three elements: (1) a single restricted night causes modest slowing, (2) the deficit compounds, and (3) five restricted nights exceed one night of total deprivation. Choice A addresses all three by showing the progression (285, 310, 362 ms) and comparing the final value (362) to total deprivation (340). Choice B only shows the first night's modest impact. Choice C discusses total deprivation in isolation. Choice D discusses the control group, which is irrelevant to the claim about restricted sleep.

distractorRationale.A: Correct: shows the compounding pattern across nights 1, 3, and 5 (285, 310, 362 ms) and demonstrates that night 5's impairment (362 ms) exceeds total deprivation (340 ms).
distractorRationale.B: Wrong (incomplete evidence): cites only the first night's modest slowing without showing the compounding effect or the comparison to total deprivation.
distractorRationale.C: Wrong (irrelevant data): discusses total deprivation's impact relative to baseline without comparing it to the cumulative restricted-sleep condition.
distractorRationale.D: Wrong (wrong comparison): describes the control group's stability, which is unrelated to the claim about cumulative impairment from sleep restriction.

imageDescription: Line graph titled 'Average Reaction Time by Sleep Condition Over Five Nights.' X-axis: Study Day (Baseline, Night 1, Night 2, Night 3, Night 4, Night 5). Y-axis: Reaction Time (ms), range 240-380. Lines: 8-Hour Control: 260, 258, 261, 259, 263, 265. 5-Hour Restricted: 260, 285, 298, 310, 338, 362. Additional marker: Total Deprivation (1 night) = 340 ms. Source: Sleep and Cognition Research Laboratory, 2024.

chartData.title: Average Reaction Time by Sleep Condition Over Five Nights
